tours.title: Architecture Overview
tours.description: A walkthrough of Pairpad's core components

steps.title: Single binary entry point
steps.description: Pairpad is a single binary with three modes: 'local' (server + daemon, opens browser), 'connect' (daemon only, remote relay), and 'relay' (server only). The main function dispatches to the appropriate subcommand.

steps.title: Local mode — zero-config
steps.description: cmdLocal() runs both the relay server and daemon in one process. It starts the server, waits for it to listen, starts the daemon, and opens the browser to the session URL via the OnReady callback. One command, no configuration.

steps.title: Protocol — message types
steps.description: All WebSocket messages are typed envelopes. This const block defines every message type: daemon→server (file operations), server→daemon (requests), browser→server (user actions), and server→browser (state broadcasts). The protocol is JSON-based and inspectable.

steps.title: Daemon — connection loop
steps.description: The Run() method connects to the relay server via WebSocket, sends the initial file tree, starts the filesystem watcher, and enters a select loop handling incoming server messages and local file change events.

steps.title: Daemon — message handler
steps.description: handleServerMessage routes messages from the server. File requests, comments, tours, and session management are all handled here. The daemon reads/writes local files and .pairpad/ data, then broadcasts results back through the server.

steps.title: Server — daemon handler
steps.description: When a daemon connects, the server creates a session with a host token, and relays messages between the daemon and browsers. File content, comments, tours, and cursor state flow through here. The server never stores code — it's a pure relay.

steps.title: Server — browser handler
steps.description: Browser connections are authenticated with a session ID and optional host token. The server enforces role-based permissions, routes messages to the daemon, and broadcasts guide/follow/cursor state to all browsers.

steps.title: Comment storage
steps.description: Comments are stored locally in .pairpad/comments.json. The commentStore manages persistence, anchor population for re-anchoring, and line range support. Comments never leave the developer's machine.

steps.title: Tour storage
steps.description: Tours are stored in .pairpad/tours.json with a runtime copy for re-anchored line numbers. The on-disk version keeps authored line numbers; the runtime version adjusts them based on current file content. Anchors are populated on save and re-evaluated on file changes.

steps.title: Editor module
steps.description: The browser IDE is built with CodeMirror 6. This module handles the editor instance, syntax highlighting, theme switching, unified gutter markers, guide highlights, peer selections, tour step markers, and editable/read-only state for role enforcement.

steps.title: Frontend entry point
steps.description: The main frontend module handles WebSocket connection, file tree, tabs, comments, guide mode, tour mode, session roles, theme toggle, and all user interactions. Bundled with esbuild into the embedded static assets.

tours.title: How to Add a Feature
tours.description: Step-by-step guide for adding a new message type and UI feature

steps.title: 1. Define the message type
steps.description: Add a new MessageType constant here. Follow the grouping: daemon→server, server→daemon, browser→server, server→browser. Then add the corresponding struct at the bottom of the file with JSON tags.

steps.title: 2. Handle it in the server
steps.description: The server's browser message switch is here. For most features, add a case that checks the sender's role (hasRole), then relay to the daemon (writeToDaemon) or broadcast to browsers (broadcastToBrowsers).

steps.title: 3. Handle it in the daemon
steps.description: If your feature involves the filesystem or local storage (like comments/tours), add a case here. The daemon reads/writes local files and broadcasts results back through the server via sendComments/sendTours.

steps.title: 4. Handle it in the frontend
steps.description: The handleMessage switch routes incoming messages. Parse the payload and call your rendering function. Keep the handler small — put the logic in a separate function below. Check myRole for permission-gated UI.

steps.title: 5. Build and test
steps.description: 'make build' compiles everything: frontend (esbuild), then the single Go binary. 'make local' runs everything and opens the browser. 'make relay' + 'make connect' for split mode development.

tours.title: Security Audit
tours.description: A guided walkthrough of Pairpad's security properties for auditors and security-conscious teams

steps.title: Minimal dependencies
steps.description: Pairpad has exactly 2 direct dependencies: coder/websocket (WebSocket protocol) and fsnotify (filesystem watcher). Plus one transitive dependency (golang.org/x/sys). The entire dependency tree is auditable in minutes.

steps.title: Hard deny list — files never served
steps.description: These patterns are always denied regardless of configuration. .env files, SSH keys, GPG keys, PEM/key files, .git directory, temp files, and node_modules are never exposed over the WebSocket connection. This list cannot be overridden.

steps.title: Path traversal prevention
steps.description: Any relative path containing '..' is rejected. Combined with filepath.Join() for all file operations, this prevents directory traversal attacks. A browser client cannot request files outside the project directory.

steps.title: File size limits
steps.description: Files larger than 5MB are silently skipped during tree scanning and file reads. This prevents memory exhaustion. The WebSocket read limit is set to 10MB on both sides.

steps.title: Wire protocol — JSON envelopes
steps.description: All messages are JSON-encoded with a 'type' field for routing and a 'payload' field. The protocol is stateless and inspectable with standard WebSocket debugging tools. No binary protocol, no obfuscation.

steps.title: Server is a stateless relay
steps.description: The Server struct holds only ephemeral session state in memory. No database, no file storage, no cache. When the server restarts, all sessions end. Code transits the server as WebSocket messages but is never written to disk.

steps.title: Role-based access control
steps.description: Three roles: host (daemon owner, authenticated via token), editor, commenter. The hasRole function gates every mutating message — the server drops messages that exceed the sender's permission level. Enforcement is server-side, not just UI.

steps.title: Comments stay local
steps.description: Comments and tours are stored in .pairpad/ inside your project directory. The daemon reads and writes them. The server only relays during live sessions. When the session ends, the server retains nothing.

steps.title: Write serialization — no race conditions
steps.description: All WebSocket writes to the daemon are serialized through writeToDaemon() with mutex protection. Browser broadcasts copy the connection list under a read lock before iterating. This prevents concurrent write corruption.

steps.title: Daemon connects outbound only
steps.description: The daemon initiates an outbound WebSocket connection to the server. No inbound ports are opened. No port forwarding, no firewall rules, no NAT traversal. Works behind corporate firewalls.

steps.title: Frontend — no tracking, no analytics
steps.description: The browser IDE is a static bundle served from the Go binary. No third-party scripts, no CDN loads, no analytics, no cookies (only localStorage for display name and theme preference). View source to verify.
